Traditionally only Japanese works have been considered anime, but some works have sparked debate about blurring the lines between anime and cartoons, such as the American anime-style productions *[Avatar: The Last Airbender](/wiki/Avatar:_The_Last_Airbender "Avatar: The Last Airbender")* and *[The Legend of Korra](/wiki/Avatar:_The_Legend_of_Korra "Avatar: The Legend of Korra")*.[[244]](#cite_note-escapist-247) These anime-styled works have become defined as [anime-influenced animation](/wiki/Anime-influenced_animation "Anime-influenced animation"), in an attempt to classify all anime styled works of non-Japanese origin.[[245]](#cite_note-whatisanime-248) Some creators of these works cite anime as a source of inspiration; for example the French production team for *[Ōban Star-Racers](/wiki/%C5%8Cban_Star-Racers "Ōban Star-Racers")* moved to Tokyo to collaborate with a Japanese production team.[[246]](#cite_note-249)[[247]](#cite_note-250)[[248]](#cite_note-company-251) When anime is defined as a "style" rather than as a national product, it leaves open the possibility of anime being produced in other countries,[[244]](#cite_note-escapist-247) but this has been contentious amongst fans, with John Oppliger stating, "The insistence on referring to original American art as Japanese "anime" or "manga" robs the work of its cultural identity."[[15]](#cite_note-anna-16)[[249]](#cite_note-252)

While some anime will depict non-Japanese characters with specific ethnic features, such as a pronounced nose and jutting jaw for European characters,[[76]](#cite_note-do1-79) there are some styles that deliberately forgo any identification of its characters with real-world ethnicities or nationalities, termed in criticism as *[mukokuseki](/wiki/Mukokuseki "Mukokuseki")* (statelessness). *Mukokuseki* characters can significantly impact the reception of a property outside of Japan.[[250]](#cite_note-CuteCult-253)[[251]](#cite_note-CBRnation-254)

A [U.A.E.](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates")-[Filipino](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") produced TV series called *Torkaizer* is dubbed as the "Middle East's First Anime Show", and is currently in production[[252]](#cite_note-Torkaizer-255) and looking for funding.[[253]](#cite_note-256)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] Netflix has produced multiple anime series in collaboration with Japanese animation studios,[[254]](#cite_note-257) and in doing so, has offered a more accessible channel for distribution to Western markets.[[255]](#cite_note-258) Similar initiatives have been enacted by the US-based streaming service Crunchyroll,[[256]](#cite_note-CrunchyOriginals-259) producing titles such as *[High Guardian Spice](/wiki/High_Guardian_Spice "High Guardian Spice")* and an adaptation of *[Tower of God](/wiki/Tower_of_God "Tower of God")*.

The web-based series *[RWBY](/wiki/RWBY "RWBY")*, produced by [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas")-based company [Rooster Teeth](/wiki/Rooster_Teeth "Rooster Teeth"), is produced using an anime art style, and the series has been described as "anime" by multiple sources. For example, *[Adweek](/wiki/Adweek "Adweek")*, in the headline to one of its articles, described the series as "American-made anime",[[257]](#cite_note-AdweekRWBY-260) and in another headline, *[The Huffington Post](/wiki/HuffPost "HuffPost")* described it as simply "anime", without referencing its country of origin.[[258]](#cite_note-261) In 2013, [Monty Oum](/wiki/Monty_Oum "Monty Oum"), the creator of *RWBY*, said "Some believe just like Scotch needs to be made in Scotland, an American company can't make anime. I think that's a narrow way of seeing it. Anime is an art form, and to say only one country can make this art is wrong."[[259]](#cite_note-rwby-262) *RWBY* has been released in Japan with a Japanese dub;[[260]](#cite_note-263) the CEO of [Rooster Teeth](/wiki/Rooster_Teeth "Rooster Teeth"), [Matt Hullum](/wiki/Matt_Hullum "Matt Hullum"), commented "This is the first time any American-made anime has been marketed to Japan. It definitely usually works the other way around, and we're really pleased about that."[[257]](#cite_note-AdweekRWBY-260)

### Media franchises

Further information: [Media mix](/wiki/Media_mix "Media mix") and [List of highest-grossing media franchises](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_media_franchises "List of highest-grossing media franchises")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Pokemon_Center_Singapore.jpg/250px-Pokemon_Center_Singapore.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pokemon_Center_Singapore.jpg)

[Pokémon Center](/wiki/Pok%C3%A9mon_Center "Pokémon Center") at [Jewel Changi Airport](/wiki/Jewel_Changi_Airport "Jewel Changi Airport") in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore")

In [Japanese culture](/wiki/Japanese_culture "Japanese culture") and entertainment, the term [media mix](/wiki/Media_mix "Media mix") refers to a strategy to disperse content across multiple representations: different [broadcast media](/wiki/Broadcast_media "Broadcast media"), gaming platforms, cell phones, toys, [amusement parks](/wiki/Amusement_park "Amusement park"), and other methods.[[261]](#cite_note-jen-264) It is the Japanese term for a [transmedia franchise](/wiki/Media_franchise#Transmedia_franchise "Media franchise").[[262]](#cite_note-Steinberg-265)[[263]](#cite_note-266) The term gained circulation in the late 1980s, but the origins of the strategy can be traced back to the 1960s with the proliferation of anime, with its interconnection of media and commodity goods.[[264]](#cite_note-amm-267)

A number of anime and manga [media franchises](/wiki/Media_franchise "Media franchise") such as *[Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba](/wiki/Demon_Slayer:_Kimetsu_no_Yaiba "Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba")*, *[Dragon Ball](/wiki/Dragon_Ball "Dragon Ball")*, *[Fate/stay night](/wiki/Fate/stay_night "Fate/stay night")*, *[Yu-Gi-Oh!](/wiki/Yu-Gi-Oh! "Yu-Gi-Oh!")*, *[Neon Genesis Evangelion](/wiki/Neon_Genesis_Evangelion_(franchise) "Neon Genesis Evangelion (franchise)")* and *[Gundam](/wiki/Gundam "Gundam")* have gained considerable global popularity, and are among the world's [highest-grossing media franchises](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_media_franchises "List of highest-grossing media franchises"). *[Pokémon](/wiki/Pok%C3%A9mon "Pokémon")* in particular is estimated to be the highest-grossing media franchise of all time.[[265]](#cite_note-licensing-268)
